texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study investigates the transport process of various chlorinated hydrocarbons through natural rubber (NR), epoxidized natural rubber (ENR), and their blends. The effect of structure and morphology of the membranes on the transport parameters has been investigated. Sorption coefficient is found to increase and the permeability coefficient shows the reverse trend with increasing epoxy content. As the mol% epoxidation increases, the polymeric network structure becomes more compact, which leads to a decrease in the diffusion coefficient, which ultimately results in a low permeation coefficient. The morphology of NR/ENR blends  has  been  investigated  by  combining  scanning  electron  microscopy with permeability measurements. The permeation coefficient is found to be minimum for the NR/ENR 70/30 composition; a 50/50 composition shows the maximum. The heterogeneous morphology of 70/30  NR/ENR  blend  results  in  the  low  permeability  coefficient, whereas the co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition accounts for
texts.text: The study investigates the transport process of various chlorinated hydrocarbons through natural rubber (NR), epoxidized natural rubber (ENR), and their blends. The effect of structure and morphology of the membranes on the transport parameters has been investigated. Sorption coefficient is found to increase and the permeability coefficient shows the reverse trend with increasing epoxy content. As the mol% epoxidation increases, the polymeric network structure becomes more compact, which leads to a decrease in the diffusion coefficient, which ultimately results in a low permeation coefficient. The morphology of NR/ENR blends  has  been  investigated  by  combining  scanning  electron  microscopy with permeability measurements. The permeation coefficient is found to be minimum for the NR/ENR 70/30 composition; a 50/50 composition shows the maximum. The heterogeneous morphology of 70/30  NR/ENR  blend  results  in  the  low  permeability  coefficient, whereas the co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition accounts for

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: INTRODUCTION
texts.text: INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The transport of a condensable vapor through a dense membrane consecutive to an activity gradient is usually referred to as the vapor permeation process. Vapor permeation offers the unique feature, from a fundamental point of view, of studying the transport process of a single permeant through a dense membrane, under various upstream concentrations (1) (activities). Such a characteristic can by no means be obtained by liquid permeation, where the modification of the upstream activity of a component can only be achieved by adding another component to the mixture. The activity of both components is modified in this case in compliance with the Gibbs-Duhem relation, which complicates the transport analysis. In the case of pure solvent vapor permeation, upstream activity can be easily calculated, provided upstream pressure is precisely monitored (2).
texts.text: The transport of a condensable vapor through a dense membrane consecutive to an activity gradient is usually referred to as the vapor permeation process. Vapor permeation offers the unique feature, from a fundamental point of view, of studying the transport process of a single permeant through a dense membrane, under various upstream concentrations (1) (activities). Such a characteristic can by no means be obtained by liquid permeation, where the modification of the upstream activity of a component can only be achieved by adding another component to the mixture. The activity of both components is modified in this case in compliance with the Gibbs-Duhem relation, which complicates the transport analysis. In the case of pure solvent vapor permeation, upstream activity can be easily calculated, provided upstream pressure is precisely monitored (2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apart from this fundamental advantage, a study of solvent vapor permeation offers a direct practical conclusion for the understanding and rational design of volatile organic components (VOC) vapor recovery from a contaminated airstream (3,4). For VOC vapor recovery as well as organics extraction from an aqueous stream by pervaporation, a membrane material showing high organic vapor permeabilities but low air and water permeabilities is required in order to set up a competitive process.
texts.text: Apart from this fundamental advantage, a study of solvent vapor permeation offers a direct practical conclusion for the understanding and rational design of volatile organic components (VOC) vapor recovery from a contaminated airstream (3,4). For VOC vapor recovery as well as organics extraction from an aqueous stream by pervaporation, a membrane material showing high organic vapor permeabilities but low air and water permeabilities is required in order to set up a competitive process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A method was developed for simultaneous determination of the permeability ( P ), diffusion ( D ), and solubility ( S ) coefficients of organic vapors through polymers (5) from the mass transfer through polymer membranes. The S and D of  acetylene,  ethylene,  ethane,  propane,  butane,  pentane,  and  xenon  in poly(trimethyl vinyl silane) were determined by the sorption-desorption method (6). Sansone and Tewari (7) calculated the permeability coefficient of vapors of benzene in air from the measured values of solubility and diffusivity for natural rubber, neoprene, a blend of natural rubber and neoprene, butyl rubber, poly(vinyl chloride), and polyethylene membranes.
texts.text: A method was developed for simultaneous determination of the permeability ( P ), diffusion ( D ), and solubility ( S ) coefficients of organic vapors through polymers (5) from the mass transfer through polymer membranes. The S and D of  acetylene,  ethylene,  ethane,  propane,  butane,  pentane,  and  xenon  in poly(trimethyl vinyl silane) were determined by the sorption-desorption method (6). Sansone and Tewari (7) calculated the permeability coefficient of vapors of benzene in air from the measured values of solubility and diffusivity for natural rubber, neoprene, a blend of natural rubber and neoprene, butyl rubber, poly(vinyl chloride), and polyethylene membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Equilibrium sorption of a vapor has been used for the thermodynamic characterization of a polymer blend (8,9). Analysis of equilibrium sorption of a vapor by the blend can provide information regarding polymer-polymer interaction. The usual technique for investigating the morphology of immiscible polymer blends is scanning electron microscopy applied to the surface of the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study intends to examine the influence of the sorption and diffusion steps in the overall permeation flux of organic vapors. Further, permeation results have been used to complement the observations from scanning electron microscopy.
texts.text: This study intends to examine the influence of the sorption and diffusion steps in the overall permeation flux of organic vapors. Further, permeation results have been used to complement the observations from scanning electron microscopy.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: EXPERIMENT
texts.text: EXPERIMENT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Indian standard natural rubber (ISNR-5) was supplied by RRII, Kottayam, India. Epoxidized natural rubber-epoxyprene with 25 mol% epoxidation (ENR25) and epoxyprene with 50 mol% epoxidation (ENR-50) were supplied by Rubber Research Institute, Malaysia. The number-average molecular weight ( M n ) of all three rubbers is in the range 1 × 10 6 to 9.9 × 10 5 . Compounding was done on a two-roll mixing mill (friction ratio 1 : 1.4), according to ASTM D15-627.  The  basic  formulation  used  is  given  in  Table  1.  Natural rubber/epoxidized NR blends were prepared by the master batch technique. The blend compositions are 70/30, 50/50, and 30/70. The compounds were then compression molded along the mill grain direction using an electrically heated hydraulic press at 150°C.
texts.text: Indian standard natural rubber (ISNR-5) was supplied by RRII, Kottayam, India. Epoxidized natural rubber-epoxyprene with 25 mol% epoxidation (ENR25) and epoxyprene with 50 mol% epoxidation (ENR-50) were supplied by Rubber Research Institute, Malaysia. The number-average molecular weight ( M n ) of all three rubbers is in the range 1 × 10 6 to 9.9 × 10 5 . Compounding was done on a two-roll mixing mill (friction ratio 1 : 1.4), according to ASTM D15-627.  The  basic  formulation  used  is  given  in  Table  1.  Natural rubber/epoxidized NR blends were prepared by the master batch technique. The blend compositions are 70/30, 50/50, and 30/70. The compounds were then compression molded along the mill grain direction using an electrically heated hydraulic press at 150°C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the rubber phases with sulfur and zinc oxide effects a large increase in its electron density. The reaction medium for the ebonite treatment consists of molten sulfur/accelerator ( N -cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazyl sulfenamide)/zinc stearate in the weight ratio 90/5/5. Small pieces of the samples are cut and are immersed in the molten sulfur mixture for 8 h at 120°C. The excess sulfur was carefully scrapped from the outer surface before thin sections were cut for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Philips, Netherlands) observations. The dimensions of the dispersed phase were calculated from the SEM micrographs by considering more than 300 domains.
texts.text: the rubber phases with sulfur and zinc oxide effects a large increase in its electron density. The reaction medium for the ebonite treatment consists of molten sulfur/accelerator ( N -cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazyl sulfenamide)/zinc stearate in the weight ratio 90/5/5. Small pieces of the samples are cut and are immersed in the molten sulfur mixture for 8 h at 120°C. The excess sulfur was carefully scrapped from the outer surface before thin sections were cut for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Philips, Netherlands) observations. The dimensions of the dispersed phase were calculated from the SEM micrographs by considering more than 300 domains.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Permeability Measurements
texts.text: Permeability Measurements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For conducting the vapor permeation experiment, small vials were filled with solvent and tightly closed by a membrane having 1-mm-thick individual polymers and blends. The weight loss of the vials with time is noted. The weight loss is proportional to time, area of the membrane, and pressure inside the vials, and it is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane.
texts.text: For conducting the vapor permeation experiment, small vials were filled with solvent and tightly closed by a membrane having 1-mm-thick individual polymers and blends. The weight loss of the vials with time is noted. The weight loss is proportional to time, area of the membrane, and pressure inside the vials, and it is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
texts.text: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeation through polymer film is a result of two distinct processes, solubility, which is a thermodynamic quantity, and diffusion a kinetic one (11-14):
texts.text: Permeation through polymer film is a result of two distinct processes, solubility, which is a thermodynamic quantity, and diffusion a kinetic one (11-14):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P = DS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where P , D , and S are the permeability, diffusion, and solubility coefficients. Permeability through a polymeric film depends on many factors: (1) external (temperature, pressure, concentration, humidity, etc.), (2) penetrant (solubility, molecular shape, weight, etc.), and (3) polymer (15-17). A detailed analysis of the transport process of a single permeant can thus be achieved, provided sorption and either diffusion or permeation can be studied independently. However, it has been suggested that effective diffusion coefficients obtained from permeability and sorption measurements are more realistic for a correct transport description (18).
texts.text: where P , D , and S are the permeability, diffusion, and solubility coefficients. Permeability through a polymeric film depends on many factors: (1) external (temperature, pressure, concentration, humidity, etc.), (2) penetrant (solubility, molecular shape, weight, etc.), and (3) polymer (15-17). A detailed analysis of the transport process of a single permeant can thus be achieved, provided sorption and either diffusion or permeation can be studied independently. However, it has been suggested that effective diffusion coefficients obtained from permeability and sorption measurements are more realistic for a correct transport description (18).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At a given temperature, pressure, and concentration, permeability through the polymer depends on the solubility of the solvent molecule into the polymer membrane and the diffusivity in it. The solubility of the penetrant generally becomes high when the difference in the solubility parameter between the polymer and the penetrant is small (19,20). However, when the polymer has a compact network structure, diffusivity of a penetrant through the polymer membrane tends to decrease (21). The description of diffusion mechanism in rubbery polymers is essentially concerned with the ability of the polymer to

The solubility of the penetrant generally becomes high when the difference in the solubility parameter between the polymer and the penetrant is small (19,20). However, when the polymer has a compact network structure, diffusivity of a penetrant through the polymer membrane tends to decrease (21). The description of diffusion mechanism in rubbery polymers is essentially concerned with the ability of the polymer to

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Effect of Epoxidation
texts.text: Effect of Epoxidation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Analysis of the permeation coefficient values show that the permeability coefficient decreases with epoxidation, whereas the sorption constants increase with epoxidation (Fig. 1). The increase in sorption constants for ENR-25 and ENR-50 can be explained by the difference in the solubility parameter between the polymer and solvent. These values given in Table 2 explain the increased sorption constant values with epoxidation.
texts.text: Analysis of the permeation coefficient values show that the permeability coefficient decreases with epoxidation, whereas the sorption constants increase with epoxidation (Fig. 1). The increase in sorption constants for ENR-25 and ENR-50 can be explained by the difference in the solubility parameter between the polymer and solvent. These values given in Table 2 explain the increased sorption constant values with epoxidation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The decrease in permeation coefficient values can be explained by the decreased diffusivity of the permeant molecules through the polymer membrane.
texts.text: The decrease in permeation coefficient values can be explained by the decreased diffusivity of the permeant molecules through the polymer membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 2 shows the variation of permeation and diffusion coefficients with epoxidation. It is seen that even though the sorption constants increase with epoxidation, diffusivity decreases. This is because of a more compact polymeric network structure in ENR. With epoxidation, Tg values increase (NR = -72°C, ENR-25 = -47°C, ENR-50 = -24°C). This increased Tg values suggests less polymeric chain mobility in ENR, which decreases the diffusivity of penetrant molecules. Another factor to be considered is the increased gel content in ENR. It is already established that ENR contains a highly crosslinked gel phase and a lightly cross-linked sol phase (23). The gel content has been estimated according to ASTMD and the value for ENR-25 is 47 ± 3%, and for ENR-50, it is 75 ± 3%. The gel phase hinders the mobility of the penetrant molecules with a consequent decrease is diffusion coefficient value.
texts.text: Figure 2 shows the variation of permeation and diffusion coefficients with epoxidation. It is seen that even though the sorption constants increase with epoxidation, diffusivity decreases. This is because of a more compact polymeric network structure in ENR. With epoxidation, Tg values increase (NR = -72°C, ENR-25 = -47°C, ENR-50 = -24°C). This increased Tg values suggests less polymeric chain mobility in ENR, which decreases the diffusivity of penetrant molecules. Another factor to be considered is the increased gel content in ENR. It is already established that ENR contains a highly crosslinked gel phase and a lightly cross-linked sol phase (23). The gel content has been estimated according to ASTMD and the value for ENR-25 is 47 ± 3%, and for ENR-50, it is 75 ± 3%. The gel phase hinders the mobility of the penetrant molecules with a consequent decrease is diffusion coefficient value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Roland and Böhm (24) and Tokita (25), during mixing of rubber blends, the dispersed domains are deformed during the passage through the high-shear regions of the mixing mill, and under such conditions, the domains will undergo breakup to form smaller particles or coalesce to form larger dispersed domains. In fact, the final morphology is an equilibration between domain breakup and coalescence. The SEM micrograph show that in both NR /ENR25 and NR/ENR-50 blends, at low volume fraction of ENR, well-dispersed, almost spherical drops are observed. With the increase in volume fraction of the dispersed phase, individual particles coalesce and, finally, most particles become very elongated and tend to form a continuous phase. The size characteristics of the dispersed phase ( D ⎯ n , D ⎯ w , D ⎯ a , and D ⎯ v ) and its distribution (polydispersity index values) in the different blend compositions are presented in Tables 3 and 4. These are calculated using the equations (26)

The solubility parameter difference between the polymer and the solvent is given in Tables 5 and 6. From the values given in the tables, it can be seen that the solubility parameter difference between the polymer and solvent decreases with increase in volume fraction of ENR. Thus, the sorption constant is a minimum for NR, which increases with volume fraction of ENR, and reaches a maximum for ENR, which is in accordance with the solubility parameter difference between the polymer and the solvent. The permeation coefficient is a composite term which involves the thermodynamic sorption constant and the kinetic diffusion coefficient. Thus, the variation in permeation coefficient values can be explained on the basis of the variation in diffusion coefficient values, which is a kinetic parameter. Figure 8 shows the lowest diffusion coefficient values for the 70/30 composition and the highest for the 50/50 composition.

Figure 8 shows the lowest diffusion coefficient values for the 70/30 composition and the highest for the 50/50 composition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These observations result from the unique morphology of NR/ENR blends. From the SEM micrograph it is seen that the 70/30 composition presents a heterogeneous nature and the 50/50 composition a co-continuous nature. The dispersed domains in the 70/30 composition offers a more tortuous path for the penetrants, thereby reducing the diffusivity through the membrane, which, in turn, results in the reduction in permeability coefficient. The co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition offers a smooth passage for the penetrants through the membrane, thereby showing increased permeability. The permeability coefficient values of the NR/ENR 30/70 composition explains the heterogeneous nature of the 30/70 composition.
texts.text: These observations result from the unique morphology of NR/ENR blends. From the SEM micrograph it is seen that the 70/30 composition presents a heterogeneous nature and the 50/50 composition a co-continuous nature. The dispersed domains in the 70/30 composition offers a more tortuous path for the penetrants, thereby reducing the diffusivity through the membrane, which, in turn, results in the reduction in permeability coefficient. The co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition offers a smooth passage for the penetrants through the membrane, thereby showing increased permeability. The permeability coefficient values of the NR/ENR 30/70 composition explains the heterogeneous nature of the 30/70 composition.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Investigation of the Blend Morphology
texts.text: Investigation of the Blend Morphology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability of immiscible polymer blends depends on their morphology. Two extreme situations can be considered corresponding to multilayers of the components either parallel or perpendicular to the direction of the permeant flux. With respect to that direction, they can be considered as fully continuous and fully discontinuous phase morphologies, respectively. If the continuous phase is the more permeable, a parallel configuration represents the limiting behavior. The permeability of such system varies with the permeabilities P 1 and P 2 of the individual components and with their volume fraction 𝛟 1 and 𝛟 2 according to equation
texts.text: The permeability of immiscible polymer blends depends on their morphology. Two extreme situations can be considered corresponding to multilayers of the components either parallel or perpendicular to the direction of the permeant flux. With respect to that direction, they can be considered as fully continuous and fully discontinuous phase morphologies, respectively. If the continuous phase is the more permeable, a parallel configuration represents the limiting behavior. The permeability of such system varies with the permeabilities P 1 and P 2 of the individual components and with their volume fraction 𝛟 1 and 𝛟 2 according to equation

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P =𝛟 1 P 1 +𝛟 2 P 2

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CONCLUSION
texts.text: CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The transport process of various penetrants through natural rubber, epoxidized natural rubber, and their blend membranes has been investigated. The analysis has shown how the different transport parameters vary according to the structure and morphology of the membrane material under study. It is found that  the  permeability  coefficient  decreases  with  epoxidation, whereas the sorption coefficient increases with epoxidation. The thermodynamic sorption constant depends on the solubility parameter difference between the polymer and the solvent, and the permeation coefficient depends on diffusion coefficient, which is a kinetic parameter. The decrease in diffusion coefficient resulting from the more compact polymeric network structure as the level of mol% epoxidation increases leads to the decreased permeation coefficient.
texts.text: The transport process of various penetrants through natural rubber, epoxidized natural rubber, and their blend membranes has been investigated. The analysis has shown how the different transport parameters vary according to the structure and morphology of the membrane material under study. It is found that  the  permeability  coefficient  decreases  with  epoxidation, whereas the sorption coefficient increases with epoxidation. The thermodynamic sorption constant depends on the solubility parameter difference between the polymer and the solvent, and the permeation coefficient depends on diffusion coefficient, which is a kinetic parameter. The decrease in diffusion coefficient resulting from the more compact polymeric network structure as the level of mol% epoxidation increases leads to the decreased permeation coefficient.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Investigation of permeability data in NR/ENR blends show that the sorption constant increases with the volume fraction of ENR. However, comparing the different blend compositions, it is seen that the NR/ENR 70/30 has the lowest permeation coefficient values and the 50/50 composition the highest. This behavior has been attributed to the heterogeneous nature of the 70/30 composition and the co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition, respectively. Comparison of the experimental permeability data with theoretical predictions confirm the co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition. Thus, in NR/ENR blends, SEM and permeability measurements are complementary sources of information about the morphology of the blends.
texts.text: Investigation of permeability data in NR/ENR blends show that the sorption constant increases with the volume fraction of ENR. However, comparing the different blend compositions, it is seen that the NR/ENR 70/30 has the lowest permeation coefficient values and the 50/50 composition the highest. This behavior has been attributed to the heterogeneous nature of the 70/30 composition and the co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition, respectively. Comparison of the experimental permeability data with theoretical predictions confirm the co-continuous nature of the 50/50 composition. Thus, in NR/ENR blends, SEM and permeability measurements are complementary sources of information about the morphology of the blends.
